explanation: etc. See also HUMAN-BREATH for less forceful breathing.

discriminators: Forceful directed air: louder, brighter and more sustained than HUMAN-BREATH, which UCS defines as the less forceful case.
Unvoiced throughout — voicing_ratio near zero separates it from every VOICES subcategory.
Longer and flatter-enveloped than HUMAN-SPIT or HUMAN-SNEEZE, which are impulsive.
Unvoiced airy noise with a soft, rounded attack — quieter and gentler than HUMAN-BLOW, which UCS explicitly reserves for the forceful case.
Panting and hyperventilating give a slow inhale/exhale onset train (~0.3–1.5 /s); slower and far less pitched than HUMAN-SNORE, which is voiced-buzzy and strongly periodic.
No glottal impulse — separates it from HUMAN-COUGH and HUMAN-SNEEZE, which are impulsive bursts.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

provenance.reason: the reasoned gate rejected 23% of this subcategory's own true clips

discriminators: Low, buzzy, gurgling eructation: a dark centroid and a strong sub-200 Hz share, unlike the bright unvoiced bursts of HUMAN-COUGH and HUMAN-SNEEZE.
Same low buzzy family as FARTS but with vocal-tract formants and a higher mid-band share; the FARTS bands sit lower still.
Longer and less sharply front-loaded than HUMAN-SPIT.

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

discriminators: Explosive glottal release: near-instant attack on top of an unvoiced noisy body, with a short throaty tail. Bursts often repeat in irregular fits, unlike the single event of HUMAN-SNEEZE.
Darker and more throat-centred than HUMAN-SNEEZE, whose nasal release pushes energy above 2 kHz.
Far spikier (high crest, high envelope skewness) than HUMAN-BREATH or HUMAN-BLOW.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

explanation: both literal as in a Dr. office

discriminators: The cleanest case in HUMAN: a slow, strongly periodic train of very low-frequency thumps at roughly 1–1.5 /s (60–90 BPM), with almost no energy above 2 kHz.
Periodicity plus the sub-200 Hz concentration separates it from every other HUMAN subcategory; onset_periodicity near 0.8 with onset_rate near 1 /s is diagnostic.
Slower and much darker than HUMAN-SNORE, which cycles at ~0.2–0.4 /s but carries buzzy mid-band harmonics.
Small, quiet, bright wet clicks — a suction transient, not a vocalization: voicing_ratio near zero and a very low absolute level.
Much shorter and quieter than HUMAN-BLOW; brighter and far less energetic than HUMAN-SPIT.
Sparser and wetter-sounding than HUMAN-SKIN friction, which is sustained rather than impulsive.

explanation: Human sounds not fitting another category in this list.

discriminators: The category's abstain target: a human-produced sound that fits no sibling. Assert only when the signal is human-plausible but no sibling's morphology wins clearly.
A liquid stream: a dense, stationary, bright stochastic texture — physically a WATER trickle, and only the human context puts it here.
Unvoiced and stationary, unlike anything in VOICES; longer and steadier than HUMAN-SPIT.
Denser and higher-onset-rate than HUMAN-BREATH, and with no inhale/exhale cycling.
Quiet, dry, high-centroid contact noise with an unstable envelope — the friction morphology, not an impulse. Rubbing, scratching and back-rubs dominate the class.
Slaps and pats are the impulsive tail of this class and blur into HUMAN-KISS; the sustained, flux-heavy rubs are what make it separable.
Unvoiced throughout, which excludes all of VOICES; drier and less bright-crackly than CROWDS-APPLAUSE, which is a dense multi-source impulse train.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

discriminators: One-shot: a short voiced/held pre-phase then a bright, nasal, hissy explosive release. The release is the brightest thing in HUMAN — high_band_energy and centroid sit above HUMAN-COUGH.
A sneeze is a single event; a cough recording usually contains an irregular fit of several. Higher onset counts favour HUMAN-COUGH.
Sharper and much louder than HUMAN-SNIFF, which is the quiet inhale with no explosive release.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

measured_evidence.caveat: Amateur Freesound audio, mono, arbitrary normalization. Treat as a prior on the prior, not as truth. Duration, level, stereo width and band limit are NOT measurable here and are left reasoned.

discriminators: A short, quiet, bright nasal inrush — a sharply time-limited unvoiced noise burst, typically repeating a few times.
Shorter, quieter and more nasal-bright than HUMAN-BREATH; no explosive release, which is what separates it from HUMAN-SNEEZE.
Direction is inward, so it lacks the long forceful outflow of HUMAN-BLOW.
A very slow, strongly periodic breath cycle (~0.2–0.4 /s, one snore per respiratory cycle) carrying a low, buzzy, rough voiced rasp around 80–150 Hz.
Periodicity plus low pitch separates it from HUMAN-BREATH, which is aperiodic and unvoiced.
Much slower and far more harmonic/mid-band than HUMAN-HEARTBEAT, which is ~1 /s and almost purely sub-200 Hz.
Lacks the 3–8 Hz syllabic modulation of speech, so it never wins against VOICES.
A wet, bright, front-loaded expulsion: a sharp unvoiced transient often followed by a small liquid splat.
Shorter and spikier than HUMAN-BLOW; unvoiced and much shorter than HUMAN-VOMIT, which is a long voiced retch plus liquid.
Louder and less nasal than HUMAN-KISS.
A two-part scene: a rough, low, voiced retch/heave followed by a wet liquid splatter — high spectral flux across a single event, with rough (inharmonic) phonation.
Longer and wetter than HUMAN-COUGH, and it carries voicing that HUMAN-SPIT does not.
The gag/heave overlaps VOICES-EFFORTS; the liquid splatter component is what tips it here.
